attributes.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with a six hour history of right eye pain, blurred vision and nausea. He is hyperopic and has no medical history. He had a similar episode two months ago with spontaneous resolution of symptoms after about an hour. On examination, his pinhole visual acuity is 6/60 in the right eye and 6/9 in the left eye. His intraocular pressure is 40mmHg in the right eye and 22mmHg in the left eye. Slit lamp examination of the right eye shows a hazy, oedematous cornea with severely injected conjunctival vessels (Image). The anterior chamber is shallow with no cells. The pupil is 4mm and does not react to light.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
The most likely diagnosis is acute angle-closure glaucoma with the stem describing a classical presentation. Acute angle closure glaucoma describes sudden onset increase in intra-ocular pressure due to obstruction of the aqueous drainage route in the eye. This usually occurs due to the peripheral iris bowing forward to the lens causing a "pupillary block".

The classical clinical presentation of acute angle closure glaucoma is a red, painful eye with associated nausea/vomiting, a brow headache, blurry vision and haloes around light. These symptoms occur due to increased intra-ocular pressure and associated corneal oedema. The examination will show a markedly increased intra-ocular pressure, a fixed mid-dilated pupil [due to pupillary block] and an associated hazy cornea. Risk factors include age >60, hyperopia [long-sightedness] and there is a 4:1 female: male ratio.

The most appropriate next step of management is acetazolamide. Intravenous acetazolamide is the treatment of choice, however oral acetazolamide is an acceptable alternative if unable to be given intravenously. The most important immediate next step in management regardless of diagnosis must aim for rapid intraocular pressure control to limit damage to the optic nerve.

Admission for ophthalmological review with appropriate symptomatic control such as morphine and ondansetron is not often performed if the intraocular pressure is controlled with oral or topical agents. Other agents that may lower intraocular pressure in addition to carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (e.g. acetazolamide) include prostaglandin analogues, β-adrenergic blockers and α-adrenergic agonists. If medical treatment is unsuccessful, surgical approaches may be considered. A peripheral iridotomy is a laser procedure that creates a hole in the iris to relieve the pupillary block. The new tract allows aqueous humor to flow from the posterior to anterior chamber.

Fluorescein staining may be considered for a red painful eye to identify corneal abrasions or ulceration, however would not provide benefit in this patient.
learningPoints: First line management of elevated intraocular pressure is intravenous or oral acetazolamide and topical anti-glaucoma agents
references: Weinreb RN, Aung T, Medeiros FA. The pathophysiology and treatment of glaucoma: a review. JAMA. 2014 May 14;311(18):1901-11. doi: 10.1001/jama.2014.3192. PMID: 24825645; PMCID: PMC4523637.
Ehlers JP, Shah CP. The Wills eye manual: Office and emergency room diagnosis and treatment of eye disease. Philadelphia: Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2016
